coredata.dc:title: Development of red fluorescent carbon dots for optical and smartphone imaging dual-mode determination of metabisulfite and pH monitoring
coredata.dc:creator: Jiao, Yuan
Wang, Bingyan
Gao, Yifang
Liu, Xiaona
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
Li, Wenjun
Qian, Tianwei
coredata.dc:description: A new type of red photoluminescent carbon dots (R-CDs) with high quantum yield of 46.9% was favorably developed by introducing the luminophore of resazurin into the framework. The obtained R-CDs exhibit excitation-independent behavior, advantageous dispersion and outstanding optical performance. It is worth noting that the proposed R-CDs demonstrate a particular response to S2O5 2- over other competitive species accompanied by a naked-eye color change from red to orange. Fluorescence quenching of R-CDs is determined to be S2O5 2- concentrations dependence with ultra-fast response time and low detection limit. The as-developed R-CDs is further employed in building a smartphone imaging colorimetry platform for portable recognition of S2O5 2- in real samples with considerable accuracy. Furthermore, the speciallyprepared R-CDs also display a significant fluorescence enhancement behavior with the increase of pH in the linear range of 5.4–6.2 with a pKa of 5.86. These findings may offer a distinctive strategy for the development of long-wavelength emission carbon dots and help to expand their application in food safety inspection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red emission carbon dots
Metabisulfite
Smartphone imaging
pH

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of S2O5 2- and anti-interference experiments
Analysis of S2O5 2- in real samples
Recovery of S2O5 2- in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM, dimensional distribution (Top left) and HR-TEM (Top right) of R-CDs. (b) FT-IR, (c) XPS survey scan, (d) C1s, (e) N1s and (f) O1s high-resolution spectra of R-CDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of R-CDs. The illustration at the top site is photos of R-CDs under sunlight (left) and UV lamp (right). (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of R-CDs at different excitation wavelengths.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs with addition of S2O5 2-. (b) The variation and (c, d) corresponding linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of R-CDs and the concentration of S2O5 2-.
Schematic illustration of R-CDs for the recognition of S2O5 2- by smartphone imaging colorimetry.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra, (b) Fluorescence lifetime, (c) Cyclic voltammogram and (d) Energy level of R-CDs, R-CDs + S2O5 2-.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs as pH decreases from 7.4 to 3.4. (b) The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs and pH.
(a) Variation in fluorescence intensity of R-CDs with time at different pH. (b) The cycle of fluorescence intensity between pH 7.4 and 3.4.
Schematic diagram of the preparation and application of R-CDs.
Detection of S2O5 2- in food samples based on fluorescence method.
Detection of S2O5 2- in commercial additives based on fluorescence method.
Detection of S2O5 2- in commercial additives based on smartphone imaging colorimetry.

head.ce:title: Development of red fluorescent carbon dots for optical and smartphone imaging dual-mode determination of metabisulfite and pH monitoring

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Transportation Holding Ecological Environment Co., Ltd, Taiyuan 030000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Transportation Holding Ecological Environment Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Transportation Holding Ecological Environment Co., Ltd, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new type of red photoluminescent carbon dots (R-CDs) with high quantum yield of 46.9% was favorably developed by introducing the luminophore of resazurin into the framework. The obtained R-CDs exhibit excitation-independent behavior, advantageous dispersion and outstanding optical performance. It is worth noting that the proposed R-CDs demonstrate a particular response to S2O5 2- over other competitive species accompanied by a naked-eye color change from red to orange. Fluorescence quenching of R-CDs is determined to be S2O5 2- concentrations dependence with ultra-fast response time and low detection limit. The as-developed R-CDs is further employed in building a smartphone imaging colorimetry platform for portable recognition of S2O5 2- in real samples with considerable accuracy. Furthermore, the speciallyprepared R-CDs also display a significant fluorescence enhancement behavior with the increase of pH in the linear range of 5.4–6.2 with a pKa of 5.86. These findings may offer a distinctive strategy for the development of long-wavelength emission carbon dots and help to expand their application in food safety inspection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Metabisulfite (S2O5 2-) has been commonly employed as an essential preservative for foods, beverages and pharmaceutical products to prevent the oxidation and growth of bacteria [1,2]. Besides, metabisulfite can conduct as an additive to inhibit the oxidation of active component in oral, topical and parenteral medicines. However, high doses of S2O5 2- can result in stimulation and damage to the bronchus and lungs, thereby causing lung cancer, cardiovascular diseases, neurological disorders, etc [3,4]. Based on this harmfulness, the threshold levels of S2O5 2- in food and pharmaceuticals are strictly controlled. S2O5 2- will be specially hydrolyzed into HSO3 - in water and the content of S2O5 2- is related to the concentration of HSO3 -. So far, some specific techniques have been developed for the determination of S2O5 2-, such as electrochemistry [5,6], chromatography [7,8] and capillary electrophoresis [9], etc. However, most of these methods may suffer from drawbacks, such as complicated pre-treatment procedures, high cost, or reliance on expensive instruments [10,11]. Strikingly, fluorescence methods have displayed fantastic advantages in improving sensitivity for trace analytes and eliminating environmental effects [12–14]. Thus, the development of highly sensitive method for trace detection of S2O5 2- based on fluorescence technology is extremely attractive in food safety inspection.

Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as fantastic nanomaterials due to unique fluorescence properties [15–18], excellent photostability [19–21] and low-toxicity [22–24]. As a novel type of fluorescent materials, CDs are extensively employed in environmental governance [25–27], electrochemistry [28–30], photocatalysis [31–33], biosensing [34–36] and other fields assigned to their specific physical and chemical properties. So far, significant effort has been devoted to constructing CDs-based luminescent nanosensors for sensitive determination of environmental pollutants, such as metal ions, anions and antibiotics, etc [37–39]. For instance, Wang et al. [40] designed a blue fluorescent CDs with abundant hydrophilic groups and graphitic nitrogen through polymerization and carbonization of norfloxacin molecules, which exhibited preferable selectivity and sensitivity for the real-time identification of trace UO2 2+ in aqueous solutions. Yang et al. [41] developed water-soluble CDs via a hydrothermal reaction and further self-assembly from hyperbranched polyethyleneimine and 2,4-dihydroxybenzoic acid, realizing the bifunctional response to trace amounts of Cu2+ and ClO- accompanied by low-toxicity and favorable cell penetration. Fu et al. [42] proposed an optical dual-mode nanosensor for the determination of TC on account of reduced state carbon dots, which provided a sensitive and on-spot sensor platform for environmental wastewater applications. Nonetheless, the quantitative fluorescence sensor based on CDs dedicated to metabisulfite has not been explored, thus the attempt to construct a CDs-based analytical platform for multimode monitoring of S2O5 2- remains a tremendous challenge.

Given the significance and difficulties in recognition of metabisulfite anions, the novel red fluorescent CDs (R-CDs) doped with luminophore of resazurin was designed for the discrimination of S2O5 2- for the first time. The R-CDs hold distinguished physical and chemical properties, and the photoluminescence of R-CDs can be effectively quenched in the presence of S2O5 2- with distinctive variation in color from red to orange. The proposed R-CDs is further successfully applied to the in-time identification of S2O5 2- through color recognizer APP on the smartphone with satisfactory accuracy. Additionally, the prepared R-CDs displayed pH-dependent behavior and the fluorescence presents a remarkable enhancement with the increaseof pH. These findings suggest that the R-CDs could be potentially employed as multi-functional nanosensors for on-site/in time application in complex environments (Scheme 1 ).

Sodium L-ascorbate was obtained from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. Resazurin and various metal salts were acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solutions with different pH values were formulated by titrating a mixed solution of 0.02 M Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 with 1.0 M NaOH or HCl. Ultrapure water (≥18.25 MΩ.cm) utilized in the whole experiment was provided by the rephile genie water system.

Typically, 0.02 g of resazurin and 0.02 g of sodium L-ascorbate were sufficiently dissolved in 20 mL EtOH. The obtained solution was sealed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 200 ℃ for 8 h. Afterwards the dispersion was filtered and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min, and the supernatant was dialyzed through dialysis membrane (MWCO = 0–500 Da). The resulting solution was filtered by microporous membrane (0.22 μm) and further rotate evaporation to obtained the solid-state R-CDs [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of S2O5 2- and anti-interference experiments
ce:section.ce:para: 50 µL of R-CDs solution (0.1 mg·mL−1) was introduced in 2.0 mL of PBS solution (pH = 7.4). Then the quantity of S2O5 2- at concentrations of 0–150 µM was introduced into as-prepared R-CDs dispersion. Each fluorescence emission spectrum was determined at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm and all operations were repeated for three times.

The anti-interference experiments were conducted by introducing the concentration of 0.5 mmol·L-1 metal ions and anions instead of S2O5 2- in mixed solution. Other measurement methods were consistent with S2O5 2-.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of S2O5 2- in real samples

ce:section.ce:para: All the food samples involved in the experiment including granulated sugar and bamboo shoots were purchased from a convenience store. The bamboo shoots were pretreated as follows: The bamboo shoot strips were cut into thin slices and ground into fine powder. 4 g of obtained powder was dispersed in 40 mL PBS solution (pH = 7.4) and stirred for 2 h, then the mixed solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. Successively, 50 µL of 0.1 mg·mL−1 R-CDs solution was introduced into 2 mL of supernatant, and the dispersions were mixed with different concentrations of S2O5 2- for further collection of fluorescence spectra.

Correspondingly, 1 g of granulated sugar was primarily dissolved in 10 mL PBS solution (pH = 7.4), the fluorescence assay was proceeded by introducing 50 µL of 0.1 mg·mL−1 R-CDs and different concentrations of S2O5 2- into 2 mL of above granulated sugar solution. Fluorescence measurement is the same as above.

The commercial additives of 96% sodium metabisulfite powder was obtained from the local supermarket. The accurately weighed 0.02 g, 0.04 g and 0.06 g of sodium metabisulfite powder were homogeneously mixed with 10 mL PBS solution (pH = 7.4). Then, 5 uL of mixed solutions and 50 µL of 0.1 mg·mL−1 R-CDs were confused with 2 mL of PBS solution in a fluorescence cuvette for further determination of fluorescence spectra.

The confused solution of R-CDs with different concentrations of S2O5 2- were configure and place in sunlight. Then the color recognition APP on smartphone was employed to evaluate the color and further convert it to the corresponding RGB value. Simultaneously, the obtained R, G, and B values were input into the smartphone software (WPS Office) to illustrate the linear relationship between R/(G + B) values and S2O5 2- concentration.

Fluorescence measurements at different pH were carried out in PBS solution (0.02 mol·L-1). 50 µL of 0.1 mg·mL−1 R-CDs solution was introduced into 2 mL of PBS solutions with different pH values of 2–13. Fluorescence emission spectra of all mixed solutions were measured and recorded in triplicate.

In the early stage of the reaction, the effects of reaction time and temperature on the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs were initially investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S1, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs culminates in the optimal value at 200 ℃ for 8 h. The morphology of produced R-CDs was investigated by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a, the R-CDs are sphere-shaped with an average size of 3.29 ± 0.27 nm, HR-TEM indicates that the lattice spacing of R-CDs is 0.22 nm, corresponding to the facet (100) of graphene. The microstructure and phase composition of R-CDs were further investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectroscopy. As presented in Fig. S2, the XRD spectrum of R-CDs demonstrates an evident broad peak at 25.5°, which is consistent with the typical vibration of graphitic carbon. The D and G bands of R-CDs at 1352 and 1600 cm−1 in Raman spectrum correspond to the disordered surface and sp2 domains framework of R-CDs. Furthermore, the integration ratio of ID/IG is determined as 0.51, indicating the defective graphite carbon of the R-CDs.

The chemical composition of R-CDs was demonstrated by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 1b, the absorption peak near 3410 cm−1 proves the O-H stretching vibration, the absorption bands at 2964 cm−1 and 2924 cm−1 are associated with C-H stretching vibration. The absorption bands at 1595 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibration of CO and -C-N, respectively. The XPS full-spectrum scan in Fig. 1c demonstrates two distinct peaks at 284.4 eV and 531.4 eV, and a faint peak at 399.0 eV, representing C1s, O1s, and N1s, respectively. Moreover, the spectra of C1s in Fig. 1d presents three functional groups, including C-C/CC (284.3 eV), C-N/C-O (286.1 eV), and CO (288.1 eV). The peaks at 398.6 eV and 400.1 eV in N1s spectra are consistent with pyridinic N and pyrrolic N respectively (Fig. 1e). The spectra of O1s demonstrates two obvious peaks at 531.3 eV and 532.9 eV, representing CO and C-O structure (Fig. 1f). The confirmation of XPS is agreement with the results of FTIR, both of which prove that R-CDs are chiefly composed of C, N and O elements, and a great quantity of carbonyl and hydroxyl groups are abundant on the surface of R-CDs.

The optical characteristics of R-CDs is performed in Fig. 2 a. The black line represents the UV–Vis absorption spectra of R-CDs with a wavelength between 200 nm and 800 nm. The absorption peak at 222 nm and 571 nm are associated with the π-π * transitions in the sp2 domains framework of R-CDs and the n-π* transition of CO and C-N. It can be seen from the comparison of UV–Vis absorption spectrum that there exist significant difference between the absorption peaks of R-CDs and resazurin (Fig.S3). The absorption peaks of resazurin at 284 nm and 380 nm apparently disappeared, and the absorption peak at 600 nm is moved to 571 nm in the UV–Vis absorption of R-CDs. Compared with the UV–Vis absorption of resazurin, the π-π * transitions in the sp2 domains framework of R-CDs accounts for a relatively high proportion, indicating that R-CDs with larger conjugate structure has been successfully prepared by the copolymerization of resazurin. The blue and red line in Fig. 2a indicate that the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of R-CDs are located at 590 nm and 606 nm. Moreover, the inset in Fig. 2a illustrates the bright red luminescence of R-CDs solution under UV irradiation (365 nm). The excitation-emission matrix of R-CDs is shown in Fig. 2b. The emission peak of R-CDs remains at 606 nm as the excitation wavelength ranged from 320 nm to 580 nm. The independent luminescence behavior may be ascribed to the surface passivation of R-CDs. In addition, the quantum yield of R-CDs was determined as 46.9% with rhodamine B as the standard (Fig. S4).

The fluorescence stability of R-CDs under different conditions was further investigated in Fig. S5. The fluorescence intensity of R-CDs remains stable upon continuous irradiation for 60 min with 365 nm xenon arc light, indicating that the R-CDs has exceptional photostability. Furthermore, no significant changes were observed in emission intensity at 606 nm at different concentrations of NaCl (0–2 mol·L-1), which certificates the remarkable salt tolerance and biostability of R-CDs (Fig. S6).

In order to verify the specific recognition to S2O5 2-, the fluorescence spectra of R-CDs was conducted towards different metal ions (including Na+, K+, Fe3+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Cd3+, Ag+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cr3+) and anions (including F-, Cl-, Br-, I-, CO3 2–, HCO3 –, ClO4 -, NO2 –, NO3 –, P2O7 4-, HPO4 2-, H2PO4 -, SO4 2-, S2-, SO3 2- and S2O5 2-). Concentrations of 0.5 mmol·L-1 different ions were added to R-CDs solution, and the effect on the optimal emission intensity of R-CDs (λem = 606 nm) is shown in Fig. S7. The histogram demonstrates that the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs is dramatically reduced only in the presence of S2O5 2-, while the influence of other competitors can be ignored. It can be seen from Fig. 3 a that the fluorescence intensity at 606 nm is significantly quenched with the increase of S2O5 2- accompanied by a slight blue-shift. The insert in Fig. 3a depicts the color comparison of R-CDs solution in the absence and presence of S2O5 2- under UV light (365 nm), demonstrating the extraordinary potential of R-CDs for visual recognition of S2O5 2-. The variation between fluorescence intensity of R-CDs at 606 nm and the concentration of S2O5 2- is fitted by DoseResp function and the related results is described in Fig. 3b. Therefrom good linear relationship can be obtained in the range of 2–48 µM and 50–90 µM, the corresponding linear equation are ΔF = 15.1522C-13.3613, R2 = 0.9990 and ΔF = 38.5915C-1192.5211, R2 = 0.9954 (ΔF = F0-F, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs at 606 nm before and after adding S2O5 2-, respectively.). The detection limit (LOD) of S2O5 2- is calculated to be 38.9 nM (Fig. 3c-d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery of S2O5 2- in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the effectiveness and practicality of as-constructed optical sensor, the recovery test was implemented to detect S2O5 2- in food samples and commercial additives. As can be seen from Table 1 , when different concentrations of S2O5 2- were introduced into food samples, the recoveries of granulated sugar and bamboo shoots are determined as 99.1%–100.6% and 99.3%–102.9%, respectively (RSD ≤ 3.70%). Furthermore, the recovery of sodium metabisulfite additives is 100.3%–102.9% with RSD ≤ 3.18%. All the results confirm that R-CDs can be available for the determination of S2O5 2- in real samples (Table 2 ).

Significantly, the color of the R-CDs solution was changed from red to orange under visible light, thus a strategy for convenient identification of S2O5 2- based on smartphone imaging has been developed. The operation process of the smartphone determination platform is demonstrated in Fig. 4 . Upon adding different concentrations of S2O5 2- in the range of 5–90 µM, the R-CDs solution display successive color change and a sequence of visible images can be captured immediately through the smartphone camera. Then the color identification APP on smartphone can estimate the colors and convert them to corresponding RGB values. The obtained R, G, and B values are input into the smartphone software (WPS Office) to confirm a linear relationship between R/(G + B) values and S2O5 2- concentration. Consequently, with the addition of S2O5 2-, the color of R-CDs solution changes from red to orange companied with the linear regression equation of R/(G + B) = -0.0421C + 7.9798, R2 = 0.9927, the LOD is determined as 1.31 μM. Thatway, the visual quantitative determination of S2O5 2- conducted by smartphone avoids the limitation of large laboratory instruments. The constructed smartphone imaging colorimetry has distinctive advantages in identifying color changes, which provides a prospect for in-time/on-site detection of S2O5 2-.

Additionally, the contents of S2O5 2- in commercial additives were determined by as-constructed smartphone sensing platform. The different concentrations of S2O5 2- (25, 50 and 75 µM) prepared by commercial additives are conflated with R-CDs solution to obtain images, the recoveries in three samples based on visible colorimetry solution ranged from 100.5% to 105.1% (Table 3 ). The above results show that the imaging colorimetry of smartphone based on R-CDs can be utilized in portable recognition of S2O5 2- in real samples with considerable accuracy.

The interaction between R-CDs and S2O5 2- was discussed in detail to comprehend the response mechanism. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum is initially conducted to investigate the structural characteristics of R-CDs before and after adding S2O5 2-. It can be seen from Fig. 5 a that the blue-shift of the absorption peak is generated from 571 to 486 nm accompanied with the color change from red to orange, demonstrating that the surface structure of R-CDs has changed remarkably [44]. As shown in Fig. 5b, the average fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs is estimated to be 3.20 ns and it decreases slightly to 2.42 ns when S2O5 2- is introduced. Moreover, the S2O5 2- possess relatively strong reducibility, which can donate electrons to R-CDs and further induce fluorescence quenching of R-CDs through charge transfer.

Furthermore, the mechanism through which the as-prepared R-CDs specific recognizing of S2O5 2- is investigated in Fig. 5c and Fig. 5d. The electrochemical properties of R-CDs are discussed by cyclic voltammetry, and the reduction (Ered) and oxidation (Eox) peaks are explored in −0.5988 V and −0.4987 V. Accordingly, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy levels of R-CDs are estimated at −3.8012 eV and −3.9013 on the basis of the empirical formula ELUMO = −e(Ered + 4.4), EHOMO = −e(Eox + 4.4). In particular, the energy gap is enlarged from 0.1001 eV to 0.2585 eV while S2O5 2- was introduced into the R-CDs solution, resulting in a blue-shift of the R-CDs fluorescence.

The fluctuations of pH is a considerable factor in water quality prediction, as well a decisive circumstance in biological function. Therefore, the optical response of R-CDs to pH is determined in Fig. S8 and the fluorescence emission spectra of R-CDs are measured under 365 nm excitation. The R-CDs exhibits a yellow–red trend under UV irradiation when the pH value increases from 2 to 13, certificating the extensive potential of implemented R-CDs as a fluorescence visualization platform for identification of pH. Moreover, the emission intensity of R-CDs at 606 nm increases with a slight red shift as the pH adjusted from acidic to neutral, indicating the pH-dependent behavior of R-CDs (Fig. 6 a). Utilizing the nonlinear DoseResp function to fit the relationship between fluorescence intensity of R-CDs and pH values, the pKa is obtained as 5.86. The linear regression equation is illustrated as y = 2547.0813x-13086.6582, R2 = 0.9968, corresponding to the linear relationship within the range of 5.4–6.2 (Fig. 6b).

The stability of R-CDs at different pH was investigated by recording the fluorescence intensity (λex/λem = 365/606 nm) for 60 min, the relevant result reveals that R-CDs has exceedingly good photostability and is suitable for pH observation in realistic environment (Fig. 7 a). Furthermore, the pH of the R-CDs solution was adjusted back-and-forth between 7.4 and 3.4 by concentrated hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide. As illustrated in Fig. 7b, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs solution can be effectively recovered after several cycles, which indicates the admirable reversibility for R-CDs.

The R-CDs exhibit a significant response to pH, and the possible mechanism may be attributed to the acid-base properties of N-containing functional groups. As a heterocyclic N-oxide dye, resazurin is selected as the luminous group doped in the structure of R-CDs [45]. On the basis of the N1s spectra of R-CDs, nitrogen atoms bonded in the R-CDs structure mainly exist in the form of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N. The process of protonation or deprotonation of nitrogen atoms eventually results in the change of fluorescence intensity.

In summary, a multi-functional R-CDs with high quantum yield was designed for the selective and sensitive determination of S2O5 2- and pH. The fabricated R-CDs has been demonstrated as a sensitive fluorescent platform for exclusively distinguish S2O5 2- accompanied by remarkable color changes from red to orange. Simultaneously, the feasibility and applicability of R-CDs for the determination of S2O5 2- were further certificated in commercial additives and food samples through the proposed fluorescence sensing method. It is confirmed that the R-CDs can be regarded as favorable fluorescent sensor for portable recognition of S2O5 2- with desirable accuracy. In addition, the fluorescence of R-CDs presented a remarkable enhancement with the increasedof pH range from 5.4 to 6.2. The developed R-CDs provide a new perspective on the construction of multi-functional application platform, which has enormous potential in food safety inspection.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province (No. 20210302124056, No. 202103021223088), Science and technology innovation project of colleges and universities in Shanxi Province (2021L029, 2021L030), Transverse research projectof Taiyuan University of Technology (RH2200002532).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.142924.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Two-photon and deep-red emission ratiometric fluorescent probe with a large emission shift and signal ratios for sulfur dioxide: ultrafast response and applications in living cells, brain tissues, and zebrafishes
Anal. Chem.

Lin, Two-photon and deep-red emission ratiometric fluorescent probe with a large emission shift and signal ratios for sulfur dioxide: ultrafast response and applications in living cells, brain tissues, and zebrafishes, Analytical chemistry 89(17) (2017) 9388-9393. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.analchem.7b02216.

Microchem. J.

J. Microbial. Biotechnol.

Synth. Met.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel fast-dried urine spot-based method for the analysis of EtS and EtG in urine by liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry
